coredata.dc:title: High luminous efficiency Au@CDs for sensitive and label-free electrochemiluminescent detection of circulating tumor cells in serum
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Pengfei
Wang, Li
Zhao, Kairen
Liu, Zhijun
Cao, Haixia
Ye, Shuying
Liang, Guoxi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, new gold@carbon dots nanoalloys (Au@CDs) were synthesized by directly heating carbon dots (CDs) and HAuCl4 aqueous solution to boiling. The as-synthesized Au@CDs exhibited 12-fold enhancement in the ECL emission compared to the single CDs. The high luminous efficiency Au@CDs was further used to fabricate an ECL cytosensor for the direct detection of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in serum by ligation of a cell-specific aptamer. Briefly, human mucin1 protein (MUC1) aptamer was immobilized onto the surface of Au@CDs on the electrode for capturing MUC1-positive MCF-7 cells (a type of CTCs). On the basis of the increased steric hindrance, the captured MCF-7 cells can be quantitatively detected. Our strategy was free from complicated separation and labeling treatment, and realized the detection of MCF-7 cells (a type of CTCs) from 100 to 10,000 cells/mL with a detection limit of 34 cells/mL, offering promising applications in ultrasensitive routine clinical analysis and point-of-care testing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au@CDs
Enhanced ECL
Circulating tumor cells
Separation-free
Label-free

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the Au@CDs-based ECL cytosensor
Characterization of stepwise fabrication of cytosensing interface
Selectivity and reproducibility of the cytosensor
Detection of MCF-7 cells in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (inset) of CDs and Au@CDs.
(a) HRTEM image of CDs; (b) STEM and elemental mapping images of Au@CDs; (c) XRD patterns of CDs and Au@CDs.

(A) ECL–potential curves of (a) CDs/GE in PBS; (b) Au@CDs/GE in PBS; (c) CDs/GE in PBS containing K2S2O8; (d) Au@CDs/GE in PBS containing K2S2O8. The PMT was set at 800 V. (B) Nyquist plots for EIS data collected from the bare or modified GE following corresponding modifications: (a) bare GE, (b) Au@CDs/GE, (c) aptamer/Au@CDs/GE, (d) MCH/aptamer/Au@CDs/GE, (e) MCF-7 cells/MCH/aptamer/Au@CDs/GE. Electrolyte: 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, biasing potential: 0.210 V.
Effect of the concentration of MUC1 aptamer (A) and capture time of cells (B) on the ΔECL responses. Error bar is based on triplicate experiments.

(A) The ECL profiles of different concentration of MCF-7 cells (0, 102, 2 × 102, 5 × 102, 103, 2 × 103, 5 × 103, and 104 cells/mL). (B) The linear regression curve of ΔECL values versus logarithm of MCF-7 cells concentration. Error bar is based on triplicate experiments.

(A) Analytical results for the detection of target cells and interferes. (Blank is the normal human serum sample, the HeLa and MCF-7 cells concentrations are 1000 cells/mL, the mixture concentration is 1000 MCF-7 cells + 1000 HeLa cells/mL). Error bar is based on triplicate experiments. (B) Repeat analytical results for detection of MCF-7 cells in serum samples (1000 cells/mL).
Schematic illustration of (A) synthesis protocol and structural model of Au@CDs (B) principle of the Au@CDs-based ECL cytosensor for CTCs detection.
Determination of MCF-7 cells in human serum (n = 3) with the cytosensor.

head.ce:title: High luminous efficiency Au@CDs for sensitive and label-free electrochemiluminescent detection of circulating tumor cells in serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, new gold@carbon dots nanoalloys (Au@CDs) were synthesized by directly heating carbon dots (CDs) and HAuCl4 aqueous solution to boiling. The as-synthesized Au@CDs exhibited 12-fold enhancement in the ECL emission compared to the single CDs. The high luminous efficiency Au@CDs was further used to fabricate an ECL cytosensor for the direct detection of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in serum by ligation of a cell-specific aptamer. Briefly, human mucin1 protein (MUC1) aptamer was immobilized onto the surface of Au@CDs on the electrode for capturing MUC1-positive MCF-7 cells (a type of CTCs). On the basis of the increased steric hindrance, the captured MCF-7 cells can be quantitatively detected. Our strategy was free from complicated separation and labeling treatment, and realized the detection of MCF-7 cells (a type of CTCs) from 100 to 10,000 cells/mL with a detection limit of 34 cells/mL, offering promising applications in ultrasensitive routine clinical analysis and point-of-care testing.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a powerful tool in analytical and clinical application with the merits of high sensitivity, low-cost instruments, and excellent controllability [1]. However, when conventional ECL reagents, such as luminol and ruthenium complexes, were used for the analysis, the single probe labeling at a single site hampers the sensitivity [2].

Over the past decades, various advanced materials and signal amplification technologies are employed to enhance the sensitivity of ECL system. In particularly, nanomaterials have attracted much attention due to their unique optical properties, large surface area and rich active sites. A variety of metallic, metal oxides, quantum dots, carbon nanomaterials and hybrid nanostructures have been utilized as signal carriers, conductors, catalysts or luminophores in ECL sensors to remarkably amplify the signals [3,4].

Among these nanomaterials, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous interest. According to previous literatures, AuNPs, which have large surface area, possess excellent electric conductivity, catalytic activity, have been successfully used as supporting materials, catalyst and conductor to amplify the ECL response [5]. CDs, a kind of novel carbon nanomaterials, have been developed as a promising ECL luminophore owing to the virtues of outstanding luminous efficiency, low toxicity, together with wonderful stability [6–8]. Encouragingly, some reports demonstrated that CDs have strong reduction capacity, and can in situ reduce Au ions to AuNPs. The formed CDs and Au nanohybrids (Au@CDs) possess higher catalytic activity [9,10], much more excellent electrochemical behavior [11,12], superior SERS effect [13,14] and enhanced biocompatibility [15] than CDs or AuNPs. It was reasonable to deduce that Au@CDs would be endowed with superior ECL properties in terms of the excellent electric conductivity, catalytic activity of AuNPs and outstanding ECL properties of CDs. However, not really much attention has been paid to the study of superior ECL property of Au@CDs till now.

Herein, we simply synthesized novel Au@CDs nanohybrids by direct heating CDs and HAuCl4 aqueous solution without adding any other reducer and stabilizer. The as prepared nanohybrids displayed enhanced ECL emission, good water solubility and easy surface functionalization (Scheme 1 A). We further made use of the high ECL efficiency of Au@CDs and developed a sensitive cytosensor that can directly detect circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in serum by ligation of aptamers (Scheme 1B). CTCs are considered as prognostic biomarkers for tumor metastasis and tumor diagnosis [16,17]. However, the number of CTCs is extremely rare in the blood samples (1 CTC per billion blood cells); this makes isolation and detection extremely difficult [18,19]. Of late, varies of technologies and methods were developed and devoted to enrich and detect CTCs [20]. For instance, microfluidic technologies, based on discriminating the size between different types of normal and cancer cells to meet the demand of CTCs separation are particularly attractive [21,22]. Nevertheless, microfluidic technologies carried out on microarrays and microfilters often suffer from primary obstacles such as abstruse techniques and destructive release, impeding the extensive practical application [23,24]. To this end, some other analytical methods have been established for CTCs detection including fluorescence imaging [25], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [26], Raman imaging [27] and so on, which is steer clear of destruction during CTCs release. However, most above methods are achieved through time-consuming experimental operations and complicated sample pretreatment procedure, hindering their promising application in clinical diagnosis. Therefore, simple and ultrasensitive approaches for the efficiently detection the trace amounts of CTCs in blood samples are in urgent demand. Our strategy was free from complicated separation and labeling treatment, and realized the detection of MCF-7 cells (a type of CTCs) from 100 to 10,000 cells/mL with a detection limit of 34 cells/mL. Overall, this work provides a viable option to prepare high luminous efficiency ECL luminophores, and presents a low cost and label-free CTCs detection strategy, offering promising applications in ultrasensitive routine clinical analysis and point-of-care testing.

Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP), 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All cancer cell lines, cell culture media and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from KeyGen Biotech. Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The MUC1 aptamer sequence (5′-SH-(CH2)6 CAGCCTGCACTCTAACGCAGTTGATCCTTTGGATAGCCTGGGTTAGA-3′) was offered by Sangon Biotech. Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Healthy human serum was donated by an affiliated hospital of Jiangsu University. All other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used throughout the experiment.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and compositional maps studies were carried out on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope (FEI, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra of nanoparticles were recorded with a UV-3600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence experiment was carried out on a RF-540 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Crystal phase was characterized by an X-ray diffractometer D8 Advance of Bruker AXS (Karlsruhe, Germany). A MPI-E ECL analyzer (Remax, China) was used to conduct the ECL measurements. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was carried out using an electrochemical analyzer (Ecochemie Co, Netherlands). All ECL and electrochemical measurements were performed with a conventional three electrode system, using a modified gold electrode (GE, Φ = 3 mm) as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and a platinum wire as counter electrode.

CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted heating method [28]. Briefly, citric acid (1 g) and urea (3 g) were dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water, followed by heating 5 min in a 1000 W domestic microwave oven. After cooling, 50 mL of ultrapure water was added to dissolve the black precipitates; the solution was then centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min to remove impurities. The brown supernatant was stored at 4 °C and diluted 40-fold with ultrapure water before use.

The synthesis procedure of Au@CDs was similar to that previously reported [15]. 5 mL of CDs aqueous solution was heated to boiling under stirring. Then 50 μL of aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (1%) was added and kept boiling for 30 min. The initially yellow color turned to finally wine red, meaning the formation of Au@CDs. After cooling, the Au@CDs suspension solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter and stored at 4 °C.

MCF-7 cancer cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium) containing 10 % FBS, and maintained in 5% CO2, 37 °C incubator. HeLa cells were cultured similarly in DMEM (Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium).

The gold electrode (GE), with diameter of 3 mm was polished with alumina slurries (1, 0.3, and 0.05 μm) and then ultrasonicated in ethanol and ultrapure water sequentially. The resulting electrode was further electrochemically cleaned in 0.5 M H2SO4 by scanning the potential between 0 and +1.5 V until a remarkable voltammetric peak was obtained.

Next, 10 μL of previously prepared Au@CDs solution was dropped on the electrode and dried under nitrogen. Then, the Au@CDs modified electrode (Au@CDs/GE) was incubated with 10 μL of 2 μM MUC1 aptamer (pretreated with 10 mM TCEP) at 4 °C for 12 h to achieve aptamer modified electrode (aptamer/Au@CDs/GE) by Au-S bond. The aptamer/Au@CDs/GE was further immersed in 1 mM MCH for 1 h to block the bare sites on electrode surface and control the orientation and spatial distribution of the aptamer [29]. The blocked electrode (MCH/aptamer/Au@CDs/GE) was then washed with ultrapure water thoroughly and prepared for the following ECL measurements.

The MCH/aptamer/Au@CDs/GE cytosensor was immersed in 1 mL of cell suspension and incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h and was then cleaned with ultrapure water. The ECL measurements were performed in 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS containing K2S2O8 (where the concentration of K2S2O8 was 0.1 M). The PMT was set at 800 V and the scanning potential was −1.5∼0 V.

Fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy were first adopted to investigate the optical properties of the prepared nanomaterials (Fig. 1 ). The synthesized CDs are strong fluorescent, and have an obvious UV–vis absorption band peaking at 350 nm similar to previous reports [28]. The formation of Au@CDs led to a characteristic plasmon absorption peak of AuNPs appeared at 594 nm, at the same time, the fluorescence decreased due to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AuNPs and CDs as shown in Fig. 1 [15].

The morphology, composition, and structure of the prepared nanomaterials were further characterized by HRTEM, STEM, element mapping and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 2 ). HRTEM and STEM results (Fig. 2A and B) demonstrate that the CDs were well-dispersed and nearly spheres having diameters smaller than 10 nm, whereas Au@CDs were flower-like in structure and had an average diameter of ∼60 nm. The corresponding element mapping analysis confirmed the presence of the basic elements C, O, N and Au in a single Au@CDs particle. In addition, the XRD profiles in Fig. 2C showed that the CDs had a broad peak centered at 2θ = 23° corresponding to the amorphous carbon atoms in plane C (002), the Au@CDs had additional peaks at 2θ = 38, 44, 64, and 77° corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of Au [30].
The results all confirmed the Au@CDs nanocomposites were successfully prepared.

To figure out the underlying ECL mechanisms of the prepared Au@CDs, the ECL performances of CDs and Au@CDs modified gold electrode (CDs/GE and Au@CDs/GE) in PBS and PBS containing K2S2O8 were explored, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, both modified electrodes showed almost no emission without K2S2O8 (curve a, b), while gave off strong ECL emissions in the presence of K2S2O8 (curve c, d), suggesting that the ECL behaviors of both CDs and Au@CDs are a co-reactant ECL process [31]. Notably, Au@CDs exhibited greatly enhanced ECL emission at the same potential compared to CDs in the presence of co-reactant K2S2O8, attributed to the AuNPs not only act as remarkable carriers to promote the loading of emitter species, but also play the role of wonderful catalysts and conductors promote the ECL reaction [32]. By optimizing the adding volume of HAuCl4, the ECL intensity of Au@CDs can achieve 12-fold enhancement compared to the CDs alone (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the Au@CDs-based ECL cytosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 outlines the designed strategy of the Au@CDs-based ECL cytosensor. CDs were first synthesized from citric acid and urea in an aqueous using a one-step microwave heating route. CDs were further used as both the reducing and stabilizing agent to synthesize AuNPs. The analyses showed that the prepared AuNPs were doped with CDs, that is, Au@CDs hybrid nanocrystals were formed. The prepared Au@CDs exhibited significantly enhancement in the ECL emission compared to the single CDs (Scheme 1A).

After, MUC1 aptamer was immobilized on the surface of the Au@CDs-coated electrode via the Au–S bond and then used to capture MCF-7 cells by specific recognition between cells and the immobilized capture aptamers. When MCF-7 cells were captured on the modified electrode surface, a significant quenching of the Au@CDs ECL signal was observed, as the cell membrane can block the ECL response between Au@CDs and the co-reactant. The quenched ECL signal reflects the concentration of the target cells (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of stepwise fabrication of cytosensing interface
ce:section.ce:para: The stepwise fabrication of the biosensor was characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 3B is the Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance curves of the electrode in each modification step. The semicircular diameter is associated with electron-transfer resistance (Ret). The bare working electrode showed a tiny semicircle domain (curve a), suggesting the electroactive ions were rapidly transported to the electrode interface. After the subsequent modified with Au@CDs, MUC1 aptamer, MCH, and MCF-7 cells, the Ret increased gradually (curves b–e) as the experiment proceeded, which was because the assembled non-conductors blocked the contact of the redox probe with the electrode surface. The EIS results suggest the successful fabrication of the cytosensor.

The principle of the cytosensor was in the basis of the MUC1 aptamer selective capture of MCF-7 cells [33]. Thus, the choice of the concentration of MUC1 aptamer and the duration of the cells capture process was critical in this study. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the ECL signal change (ΔECL) before and after MCF-7 cells addition monotonically increased with increasing the concentration of the aptamers from until reaching the plateau phase at 2 μM after 1.5 h incubation, hence this concentration was chosen for further analysis. From Fig. 4B, we can see that within a capture time span of 0–2.5 h, the ΔECL increased at the beginning and stabilizes at 1.5 h. Thus, 1.5 h was chosen as the optimized capture time in subsequent cell experiments.

Under optimum conditions, the performance of the proposed ECL cytosensor for the detection of different concentrations of MCF-7 cells was investigated. As shown in the Fig. 5 A, the ECL signal gradually decreased with increasing the concentrations of MCF-7 cells. There was a good linear relation between the ΔECL (ΔECL = E0 − E, where E is the ECL value at different cell concentrations and E0 is the ECL response without cells) and logarithm of MCF-7 cells concentration (lgC) within the range of 100–10,000 cells/mL (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 34 cells/mL by using 3S/N that is lower than that of many previous reports (Table S1). The result indicates the fact that the cytosensor can detect MCF-7 cells at a very low level. In comparison with most reported methods for CTCs detection, the present approach is much easier without the requirement of separation and labeling procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of the cytosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selectivity, the proposed cytosensor is exposed to different samples: blank serum sample, HeLa cells (MUC1 negative), MCF-7 cells, and a mixture of the two cells. The ECL results are shown in Fig. 6 A. The ECL signal was only changed in the presence of target MCF-7 cells, while no detectable ΔECL was observed in the absence of MCF-7 cells. The interference in serum was further investigated with EIS. As shown in Fig. S2, when the sensor was incubated with MCF-7 cell, the Ret increased significantly compared to that incubated the blank serum and PBS, owing to the reason that aptamer selectively capture MCF-7 cell. In contrast, no significant differences were observed in the blank serum and PBS. Thus, the proposed ECL cytosensor would provide a selective detection for target CTCs.

Moreover, the reproducibility of this cytosensor was analyzed by repeated detection of 1000 MCF-7 cells/mL for 5 times. As shown in Fig. 6B, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8 % confirmed good reproducibility of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MCF-7 cells in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prove the potential application of the fabricated ECL sensor for CTCs detection in real sample, human serum samples spiked with three different concentrations of MCF-7 cells was detected. The serum samples were used directly without any pretreatment. The recoveries ranged from 92.6% to 116.5% for the three different concentrations of cells were obtained. All above results demonstrated that the proposed cytosensor could be applied for detection of CTCs in the complicated serum matrix (Table 1 ).

In summary, novel Au@CDs with high ECL efficiency were prepared by a simple method and were successfully applied for the sensitive detection of CTCs. The as-prepared Au@CDs nanohybrids exhibit excellent ECL lminous efficiency and were easy surface functionalization. With the aid of aptamer recongnition, the proposed method can measure MCF-7 cells with a detection limit as low as 34 cells/mL. Besides high sensitivity, this method also possesses wide dynamic, good selectivity, and satisfied reproducibility, having a great potential to be applied in clinical diagnosis. Moreover, compared to the methods that require labeling and separation, the detection process of this method is simple, and is easy to be applied in point-of-care detection. Nevertheless, one disadvantage of the developed strategy is there is the mutual interference when MCF-7 and other MUC1-positive cells coexistence. Thus, future work should be focused on improving the probe selectivity. We believe that the successful application of high luminous efficiency Au@CDs will inspire the development of novel sensitive ECL platform in many fields including clinical diagnostic, environmental monitoring and food analysis in the future.

Pengfei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Li Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Kairen Zhao: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Zhijun Liu: Software, Writing - review & editing. Haixia Cao: Writing - review & editing. Shuying Ye: Writing - review & editing. Guoxi Liang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (Nos. 10JDG052, 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128131.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Sun, H. Sun, Z.Z. Liang, Nanomaterials in electrochemiluminescence sensors, Chem. Electro. Chem. 4 (2017) 1651–1662. https://doi.org/10.1002/celc.201600920.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reduced state carbon dots as both reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles

Piao, Nafion coated Au nanoparticle-graphene quantum dot nanocomposite modified working electrode for voltammetric determination of dopamine, Inorg. Chem. Commun. 105 (2019) 174–181. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2019.05.009.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mocellin, U. Keilholz, C.R. Rossi, D. Nitti, Circulating tumor cells: the ‘leukemic phase’ of solid cancers, Trends Mol. Med. 12 (2006) 130–139. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molmed.2006.01.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Circulating tumour cells: their utility in cancer management and predicting outcomes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.G. Krebs, J.M. Hou, T.H. Ward, F.H. Blackhall, C. Dive, Circulating tumour cells: Their utility in cancer management and predicting outcomes, Ther. Adv. Med. Oncol. 6 (2010) 351−365. https://doi.org/10.1177/1758834010378414.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Circulating tumor cells as “liquid biopsies” to understand cancer metastasis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Woo, M. Yu, Circulating tumor cells as “liquid biopsies” to understand cancer metastasis, Transl. Res. 201 (2018) 128–135. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trsl.2018.07.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Micro/nanofluidic technologies for efficient isolation and detection of circulating tumor cells

Chem. 89 (2017) 5966−5975. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.7b00497.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared fluorescent Ag2S nanodot-based signal amplification for efficient detection of circulating tumor cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ding, C. Zhang, X. Yin, X. Cao, M. Cai, Y. Xian, Near-Infrared Fluorescent Ag2S Nanodot-Based Signal Amplification for Efficient Detection of Circulating Tumor Cells, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 6702−6709. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.8b00514.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengfei Liu is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Li Wang received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include multifunctional nanomaterial and their applications in diagnosis, metastasis, and treatment of cancer.

Kairen Zhao is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Zhijun Liu is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Haixia Cao is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Shuying Ye presently pursuing her master degree in environmental engineering in Jiangsu University. Her research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Guoxi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor and nanofabrication.
